draft.introduction: Iliad 6:440–502 presents the farewell of Hector and Andromache, the most emotionally devastating scene in the poem. Hector chooses honor and duty knowing it will cost him his life and leave his wife and son defenseless.

translation.text: Andromache came close beside him and said through her tears, ‘Hector, your own great strength will be your death, and you have no pity on your little son, nor on me, ill-starred, who soon will be your widow. For all the Achaeans will set upon you and kill you. It would be far better for me to go beneath the earth if I lose you.’
translation.original_text: δαιμόνιε φθίσει σε τὸ σὸν μένος οὐδ’ ἐλεαίρεις παῖδά τε νηπίαχον

translator_notes.note: This scene achieves its power through Hector’s full awareness that Troy will fall. Unlike Achilles, who fights for glory, Hector fights knowing he will lose. The baby Astyanax shrinking from his father’s plumed helmet is Homer’s most famous image of war’s human cost.
